capabilityFacts.value: Runway supports text-to-video on multiple in-house and third-party models.
capabilityFacts.evidenceText: The models guide lists text-to-video for Gen-4.5, Veo, Kling, and Sora families.

capabilityFacts.value: Image-to-video is supported in both the product and the API.
capabilityFacts.evidenceText: The API reference has a dedicated image_to_video endpoint, and the models guide maps image-to-video across Runway and third-party models.

capabilityFacts.value: Runway supports video-to-video editing and stylization through Aleph and older Gen-3 paths.
capabilityFacts.evidenceText: The models guide lists Gen-4 Aleph for video-to-video edits, and Runway help docs keep a dedicated video-to-video path for Gen-3 Alpha and Turbo.

capabilityFacts.value: Act-Two and selected third-party models support performance-driven character animation.
capabilityFacts.evidenceText: The models guide lists Act-Two as character performance and also exposes WAN2.2 for reference-video character animation.

capabilityFacts.value: Lip Sync accepts image or video visuals and audio or text scripts, with up to four animated faces per output.
capabilityFacts.evidenceText: The Lip Sync guide documents supported input types, max speakers, and resolution caps.

capabilityFacts.value: Generated videos can be upscaled to 4K from the Gen-4 creation flow.
capabilityFacts.evidenceText: The Gen-4 creation guide includes an Upscale to 4K action from generated results.

capabilityFacts.value: Uploaded assets are private by default unless a user deliberately changes asset privacy.
capabilityFacts.evidenceText: The security standards article explicitly says uploaded assets are automatically set to private.

capabilityFacts.value: Unlimited-plan Explore Mode can slow down and restrict simultaneous generations based on system load.
capabilityFacts.evidenceText: The Unlimited plan details say Explore Mode runs at a relaxed rate, can take longer, and has simultaneous generation limits.

capabilityFacts.value: Veo 3 and Veo 3.1 do not use Explore Mode and require credits even on Unlimited.
capabilityFacts.evidenceText: The Unlimited plan article explicitly excludes Veo 3 and Veo 3.1 from Explore Mode.

modelSupportItems.evidenceText: The available models guide identifies Gen-4.5 as the latest Runway video generation model.
Runway describes Aleph as the video editing and stylization model for video-to-video edits.
The models guide labels Act-Two as the Runway model for character performance.

modelSupportItems.scope: Third-party video generation with or without audio
modelSupportItems.evidenceText: The API reference and models guide both list Veo 3.1 among accepted model values for image-to-video and text-to-video workflows.

modelSupportItems.scope: Third-party video generation with audio and speech
modelSupportItems.evidenceText: The models guide lists Sora 2 Pro under OpenAI third-party models with text-to-video and image-to-video support.

modelSupportItems.scope: Admins can enable or disable individual third-party models per organization or workspace
modelSupportItems.evidenceText: Runway's enterprise guide explains that admins can grant or revoke model access globally or per workspace.

uniqueValueItems.summary: Runway is not just selling one flagship generator; it is selling a creative operating surface that mixes in-house and third-party models under one workspace and API.
uniqueValueItems.evidenceText: The official materials spend significant space on model catalogs, third-party provider options, and organization-level model governance.

uniqueValueItems.summary: The product differentiates by pairing generation with transformation tools such as Aleph, Act-Two, Lip Sync, keyframes, and upscale rather than stopping at raw generation.
uniqueValueItems.evidenceText: The Gen-4 and model guides repeatedly connect generation with follow-on editing and transformation actions inside the same session.

differentiationItems.positioning: generator-led creative platform with editor and model-router characteristics
differentiationItems.evidenceText: Runway exposes more model choice, provider control, and transformation tools than a simple template-first editor.

differentiationItems.positioning: Model ceiling, controllable generation, and creative workflow breadth are stronger than low-cost volume or rigid templates.
differentiationItems.evidenceText: The official pages emphasize realism, cinematography, stylization, prompt control, and model access rather than fixed business templates.

collaborationItems.value: Enterprise organizations can manage multiple workspaces, move credits, and create reporting views from a shared organization surface.
collaborationItems.evidenceText: The enterprise organization guide lists user management, credit movement, workspace creation, and basic reporting from Organizational Spaces.

collaborationItems.value: Enterprise admins can purchase and allocate additional seats across workspaces.
collaborationItems.evidenceText: The organization guide explains how purchased seats and overages are handled when members are added.

collaborationItems.value: Enterprise admins can constrain sharing to members with workspace access when desired.
collaborationItems.evidenceText: The organization guide explicitly describes limiting asset sharing to users with access to the relevant workspace.

developerItems.value: Runway offers a versioned REST API with API-key authentication and task endpoints.
developerItems.evidenceText: The API reference documents Bearer auth, explicit API version headers, and task-based generation endpoints.

developerItems.value: Runway separates API access into Build and Enterprise packaging for internal products or large-scale services.
developerItems.evidenceText: The API overview page markets Build for individuals or teams and Enterprise for larger organizations embedding Runway into products or services.

developerItems.value: API callers explicitly choose a model per request.
developerItems.evidenceText: The image_to_video endpoint requires a model value and lists accepted model names including Runway and third-party options.

commercialItems.value: Runway publicly states SOC 2 Type II certification.
commercialItems.evidenceText: The security standards article explicitly states SOC 2 Type II certification and points enterprise users to the Trust Center.

commercialItems.value: Unlimited plan users can create infinite generations in supported modes without spending credits, but at a relaxed rate.
commercialItems.evidenceText: The Unlimited plan guide explains Explore Mode semantics and its differences from faster Credits Mode.

limitationItems.value: Costs vary substantially by model and resolution, especially across third-party models.
limitationItems.evidenceText: The models guide lists materially different credit burn rates for Gen-4.5, Veo, Kling, Sora, and image models.

limitationItems.value: Lip Sync supports up to 40 seconds per dialogue and up to 10 dialogues.
limitationItems.evidenceText: The Lip Sync guide lists both duration and dialogue caps.

limitationItems.value: Per-model enable or disable controls for third-party models are not a standard self-serve control on basic plans.
limitationItems.evidenceText: The model enablement article is framed for enterprise admins through Organization Space settings.

useCaseItems.fit: Strong fit when realism, cinematography, and model choice matter more than template speed.
useCaseItems.evidenceText: Runway's model docs repeatedly emphasize realism, cinematography, stylization, and creative control.

useCaseItems.fit: Aleph, Lip Sync, retiming, and upscale make Runway suitable when generated media must be refined rather than only created from scratch.
useCaseItems.evidenceText: The Gen-4 and Aleph materials connect video generation with edit, stylize, expand, lip sync, and upscale actions.

useCaseItems.fit: The API is strong when teams need direct model access inside their own product or internal creative tooling.
useCaseItems.evidenceText: Runway markets its API to teams adding its latest models to projects, internal products, products, and services.

userFitItems.fit: Good fit for advanced solo creators who want model choice and can manage credit tradeoffs.
userFitItems.rationale: Runway's surface rewards users who care about generation controls and model selection, not only simplicity.
userFitItems.evidenceText: The official docs are model- and prompt-heavy, which suggests a higher technical ceiling than template-first tools.

userFitItems.fit: Moderate fit for creative teams that can tolerate credit accounting and want shared creative tooling.
userFitItems.rationale: Build, credits, and model catalogs are team-usable, but some governance controls only appear at enterprise level.
userFitItems.evidenceText: API Build and workspace plans exist for teams, while finer controls are pushed into enterprise organization features.

userFitItems.fit: Strong fit when centralized model governance, seat allocation, reporting, and security reviews matter.
userFitItems.rationale: Organization spaces, trust-center artifacts, and model enablement controls are enterprise-facing.
userFitItems.evidenceText: Runway's enterprise help center dedicates separate articles to organization spaces, SSO, and model controls.

rawEvidenceBlocks.evidenceText: The model catalog includes both proprietary Runway models and third-party models from Google, OpenAI, Kling, Alibaba, and others, each with modality and credit rates.
The API is versioned and model-driven: callers authenticate with an API key and specify the target model in the request payload.
Unlimited mode is not unlimited performance; Runway explicitly warns about slower speeds and concurrency limits under Explore Mode.
Runway states SOC 2 Type II and private-by-default media handling on its public help center.
Enterprise admins can govern workspaces, credits, seats, and third-party model availability from organization settings.

unresolvedQuestions.question: Which specific SSO protocols and identity providers are publicly documented for enterprise accounts?
unresolvedQuestions.reason: The enterprise help center lists SSO resources, but a single concise public capability matrix was not captured in this pass.

unresolvedQuestions.question: What are the default output rights and commercial usage terms per model family, especially for third-party providers?
unresolvedQuestions.reason: The reviewed pages document model access, moderation, and billing, but not a consolidated rights matrix per provider.
